programs.title: Mass Loss in Massive, Evolved Stars
programs.body: The mass loss mechanisms of evolved, massive stars remain poorly understood. Do these stars primarily lose mass in steady winds, or dramatic eruptions? What are the drivers of late-stage stellar outbursts? What role do binary companion objects play in shaping the lives of these stars? Signatures of dusty outbursts in red supergiants like Betelgeuse and new observations of low-luminosity variability in supernovae – including “precursor” outbursts that announce the ultimate death of these stars – have motivated new theoretical work on both wind-driven and eruptive mass loss for single and binary systems. The coming years are expected to bring an exponential increase in such observations, thanks to missions/surveys like Rubin, DSA-2000 and Roman. This program aims to bring together experts in the internal and external lives of massive stars to better understand how mass loss episodes shape the lives of massive stars and the traces they leave in the time-domain sky.

programs.title: Small-Scale Probes of Dark Matter
programs.body: The fundamental nature of dark matter remains largely unconstrained on sub-galactic scales, though early results from strong lensing and stellar streams are now exploring the regime of million-solar-mass dark subhalos. While initial constraints appear in tension with standard CDM predictions, there has been little exploration into how to combine or test the consistency of different probes of small-scale structure. The coming years are expected to bring a transformative increase in our ability to test dark matter on small scales, thanks to unprecedented wide-field kinematics and high-resolution imaging from surveys like Rubin, DESI, Via, JWST, and Roman. The goal of this program is to bring together experts in strong lensing, stellar stream modeling, and dark matter microphysics to advance these small-scale probes and work toward joint constraints.

programs.title: Cosmic Baryons
programs.body: The distribution of cosmic baryons is a critical question that impacts astrophysics and cosmology, made difficult by the historic absence of direct measurements. In recent years, new observational signatures of the diffuse baryons in dark matter halos and beyond have offered insights into astrophysical feedback. Advances in X-ray and CMB surveys, Fast Radio Bursts and weak lensing are beginning to point to strong feedback scenarios in which supermassive blackholes play an outsize role redistributing gas at Mpc scales. Each of these empirical probes requires large hydrodynamical simulations to provide context for physical interpretation. This ITC program will provide a venue for different perspectives to cross the boundaries of wavelength and methods. The goal is to discover new synergies across sub-fields and share common pathways for data inference.
